narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and a middle-class family background, you've put real effort into your law school journey, and I appreciate how challenging it can be to hear that your application has been rejected. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and for you, it estimated a 71.1% chance of that outcome, which unfortunately crossed the threshold for denial to protect the school's bar passage rates. This prediction wasn't a judgment on your character or potential, but a data-driven assessment based on patterns from thousands of past students. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to the typical mid-range performance, and your Year 1 decile, also at the lowest level well below average. These early and later academic standings in your school stood out as the strongest signals, suggesting challenges in coursework that historically correlate with bar struggles for students like you. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, as it's close to the dataset average and pushed the model toward seeing you as more likely to pass. Your LSAT score of 28, though below the strong average of around 37, had a smaller positive nudge toward success, as did your plan to attend full-time, which aligns with the vast majority of successful students. Family income in the middle bracket had minimal sway overall. Compared to typical admitted students, who often enter with higher decile rankings in their school years and stronger test scores, your profile highlighted those early academic hurdles as the key gaps. This doesn't define your future—many overcome similar starts through targeted prep. I recommend focusing on boosting your academic deciles in any remaining coursework, retaking the LSAT for a higher score, and seeking bar-specific tutoring or study programs proven to lift passage rates. You're capable of turning this around, and with these steps, you could reapply stronger next cycle. Keep pushing forward.
